elements.sent-2411-003: We study a dynamic coloring of the vertices of a graph $G$ that starts with an initial subset $S$ of blue vertices, with all remaining vertices colored white.

elements.sent-2411-010: While the zero-forcing number of claw-free cubic graphs was studied earlier, for each pair of values $p$ and $q$ that are not both $1$ we either determine the $(p,q)$-spreading number of a claw-free cubic graph $G$ or show that $¥¥sigma_{(p,q) (G)$ attains one of two possible values.}
elements.sent-2411-011: In this paper we continue the study of dynamic graph colorings and explore the concept of $(p,q)$-spreading in claw-free cubic graph.
elements.sent-2411-012: The concept of spreading in graphs is a generalization of the well-studied concepts of $k$-forcing and $r$-percolating sets in graphs.
elements.sent-2411-013: Consider a dynamic coloring of the vertices of a graph $G$ that starts with an initial subset $S$ of blue vertices, with all remaining vertices colored white.
elements.sent-2411-014: For $k ¥¥in ¥¥mathbb{N}$, the color change rule in $k$-forcing is defined as follows: if a blue vertex $u$ has at most‾$k$ white neighbors, then all white neighbors of $u$ are recolored blue.

elements.sent-2411-016: The initial set $S$ of blue vertices is a $k$-forcing set for $G$ if by repeatedly applying the color change rule in $k$-forcing all the vertices of $G$ are eventually colored blue.

elements.sent-2411-320: Let $T$ be an arbitrary triangle in $G$.
elements.sent-2411-321: Initially, we let $S = V(T)$, and so the vertices in $T$ form the set of initially infected vertices.
elements.sent-2411-322: We now enlarge the set $S$ as follows.
elements.sent-2411-323: Suppose that $T$ belongs to a diamond-unit $D$.
elements.sent-2411-324: In this case, we let $v$ be the vertex in the diamond-unit $D$ that is not in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-325: The vertex‾$v$ immediately becomes infected in the $(2,1)$-spreading process, since both of its neighbors in $T$ have no other uninfected neighbor.
elements.sent-2411-326: As a result, all vertices in the diamond-unit $D$ become infected, that is, $D$ becomes an infected unit.
elements.sent-2411-327: Suppose next that $T$ is a triangle-unit of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-328: Then $T$ is already an infected unit.
elements.sent-2411-329: Finally let $T'$ be the infected unit containing $T$ (that is, $T'=D$ or $T'=T$).
elements.sent-2411-330: We now extend the set $S$ in the same manner as in the proof of Theorem‾¥¥ref{thm:2percolation .}
elements.sent-2411-331: Let $U$ be a unit adjacent to‾$T'$.
elements.sent-2411-332: If $U$ is a triangle-unit, then we add to $S$ a vertex in $U$ that is not adjacent to any vertex in the unit $T'$.
elements.sent-2411-333: If $U$ is a diamond-unit, then we add to $S$ a vertex in $U$ that is a dominating vertex of the unit $U$.
elements.sent-2411-334: Proceeding analogously as in the proof of Theorem‾¥¥ref{thm:2percolation , this results in all vertices of the unit $U$ becoming infected in the $(2,1)$-spreading process.}
elements.sent-2411-335: Continuing in this way by considering a vertex not yet infected and is adjacent to an infected triangle, we obtain a $(2,1)$-spreading set $S$ of $G$ starting with the set $V(T)$ and adding exactly one vertex from every unit of $G$ that does not contain the triangle $T$.
